text: Hello, I am sharing an attempted appendectomy for a patient who had symptoms for about forty eight hours. White blood cell count was in the high teens and CAT scan showed the appendicitis with a fecal lift and surrounding inflammatory reaction.
The appendix is visually adhered to the right pelvic brim, partially covered by the terminal ileum.
The surgeon visually assesses the surgical field, noting that the anti-mesenteric fat of the ileum and the cecum are covering the base of the appendix, which appears to be positioned retrocecally.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the right colon and cecum to improve visibility of the surgical area. Three laparoscopic ports are in use: an umbilical Hassan port, a 5mm suprapubic port, and a 5mm port in the left lower quadrant.

The surgeon encounters difficulty with the appendix being firmly adhered to the pelvic brim, impacting the ability to mobilize the cecum from the sidewall. The surgeon begins to chip away at the adhesion while ensuring the ureter remains distant from the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the roof of the inflamed tissue covering the appendix base, which is obscured by a fecal lift, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon begins to mobilize the appendix, revealing the necrotic and inflamed wall of the appendix body. Pus is visible as it starts to emerge from the appendix.

The surgeon cleans pus from the inflamed area and opens the tissue to access the natural plane, which begins to separate more easily. The cecum is further mobilized to improve visibility of the appendix base, which remains obscured beneath surrounding structures.
The surgeon observes the inflammatory reaction extending into the retroperitoneum while lifting the cecum to visualize the retroperitoneal attachment of the appendix to the cecum.
The surgeon works on the medial surface of the cecum, searching for the anti-mesenteric fat to approach the base of the appendix, which remains obscured.

The surgeon decides to change the approach and begins taking the mesoappendix, aiming to reach the base of the appendix. The goal is to find the open lumen, amputate the appendix, and suture the hole closed, contingent on the suitability of the cecum.

The surgeon carefully dissects the mesoappendix, avoiding the terminal ileum and cecum. A clear plane is established, and the appendix disintegrates, revealing a fecalith. The appendix is then removed along with the fecalith.

The surgeon examines the open lumen in the cecum, observing a small portion of the residual appendix. The area of concern is noted to be adjacent to the insertion point of the terminal ileum into the cecum.

The surgeon examines the ratty cecum, identifying the opening and carefully removing part of the wall to access healthier cecum tissue. The goal is to place sutures without affecting the terminal ileum (TI) and ensure they hold in the healthy cecum.
The surgeon examines the defect in the cecum, assessing the surrounding tissue quality, which appears unsuitable for suture closure due to its proximity to the terminal ileum.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, revealing a window where the terminal ileum mesentery and cecum have already been mobilized.
The surgeon isolates the iliocolic pedicle using three laparoscopic ports, with the suprapubic port upsized to twelve millimeters for stapling. The surgical field is visible without additional ports.
The surgeon separates the ileocolic artery and vein, focusing on the artery, in preparation for extraction.
The surgeon uses a ligature to take the small bowel mesentery, which appears thick and inflamed due to an inflammatory reaction.

The surgeon carefully dissects up to the bowel wall, taking the colon mesentery while preserving the rest of it. The dissection occurs above the cecum at the junction with the ascending colon, removing some of the overlying lateral attachments.

The surgeon divides the terminal ileum using a stapler inserted through the suprapubic port. The hepatic flexure is mobilized before stapling the cecum and ascending colon. The camera is positioned at the suprapubic port while the stapling is performed from the umbilical port.
The surgeon performs an additional firing of the stapler and places the resected specimen above the liver.
The surgeon aligns the colon for an isoperistaltic anastomosis, creating an enterotomy and a colotomy. The stapler is introduced through the suprapubic port to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon uses a stay suture to align the bowel onto the stapler, ensuring an anti-mesenteric to anti-mesenteric orientation to create a common channel. The common channel is then closed using a 3-0 V-Loc suture.

I was happy with my decision to do this. I did have a lot of concerns at the time of the surgery that leaving that necrotic cecum and trying to put sutures right next to the ilium where the terminal ilium entered the colon would not hold and the patient could have a little leak from that. And so I chose to do the ileocolic resection. It's only the second time in my career that I've done this. I feel like most of the time it is not necessary. It is something that I talk about and counsel my patients about prior to any appendectomy.

In retrospect, I wonder if I could have gotten away with just closing the defect, but I'm overall very happy with this, leaving nothing behind other than very healthy tissue. I did not leave a drain. I did give the individual twenty-four hours of antibiotics. This was a postoperative day one discharge, and they are healing well in the early postoperative period at this time.

I feel like this is something that does happen over the course of the career of those of us who take general surgery call. It is something that we should be prepared for and if it occurs I think it's something that we should be able to handle minimally invasively without opening and I think that doing intracorporeal anastomosis is a really nice way of handling it so that we don't need to make a peri-umbilical incision which has a higher risk of infection and long-term hernia formation.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis, revealing a flat mesentery. The omentum is positioned, and the surgical area is cleaned up.

The surgeon observes no bleeding from the staple line where the appendix was previously adhered. A quick check of the ureter is performed, followed by the extraction of the appendix specimen through an upsized suprapubic port measuring approximately two and a half to three centimeters.
